metadata.career_relevance: Until now your programs have been static — you hardcoded all the data. Real programs RESPOND to users: they ask, take input, react. Even backend services (REST APIs, message consumers) are 'just' Scanner-style input loops behind the scenes — they read a request, process it, send a response. Scanner is your first taste of that pattern. The newline-consumption gotcha you'll learn here is one of those Java idioms that trips up developers FOR YEARS if not understood — get it now and you'll save yourself debugging hell.

hook.real_world_problem:
Every Java program you've written so far has had the data baked in. `int age = 28;` Hardcoded. But that's not how real software works. When you log in to a banking app, you don't recompile the app to change your password — you TYPE it. When you order food, you don't edit source code to add an item — you select from the menu. Real programs read input from users.

In Java, the standard way to read keyboard input is the `Scanner` class. It's been in Java since 2004 and is in every textbook, every interview prep, every codebase that ever asked a console question. You'll use it constantly in Module 1-6 exercises and projects.

This topic also covers Java's most notorious newcomer gotcha: the `nextInt() + nextLine()` newline bug. Almost every Java beginner hits this within their first week. Today you'll learn what it is and how to dodge it permanently.
hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll be able to: write programs that ask the user questions and react to answers, read both numbers and text, prompt the user with a clear inline cursor (no extra newline), recognise and fix the newline-consumption bug, and close the Scanner properly. You'll build a complete interactive mini-app — a 'Loan Eligibility Checker' that takes the user's income, age, and credit score and tells them whether they qualify.

common_mistakes: Forgetting the import entirely → 'cannot find symbol: class Scanner'
Typing `import java.util.scanner;` (lowercase) → 'package java.util.scanner does not exist'
Putting the import INSIDE the class body → syntax error

reading_strings.intro:
Scanner offers two methods to read text:

**`nextLine()`** — reads ONE WHOLE LINE (everything until the user presses Enter). Returns a String.

**`next()`** — reads ONE TOKEN (one 'word' — stops at whitespace). Returns a String.
reading_strings.rule_of_thumb: Use `nextLine()` for things that might contain spaces (names, addresses, messages). Use `next()` for single-word input (usernames, tags, command words).

reading_numbers.intro:
For numeric input, Scanner has typed methods that convert text → number for you:

| Method | Returns | Reads input like |
|--------|---------|------------------|
| `nextInt()` | int | `42` |
| `nextLong()` | long | `5234891753` |
| `nextDouble()` | double | `19.99` |
| `nextFloat()` | float | `3.14f` (no f suffix needed when typing) |
| `nextBoolean()` | boolean | `true` or `false` |
reading_numbers.what_can_go_wrong: If the user types text where a number is expected (e.g. types `hello` when `nextInt()` is waiting), Java throws `InputMismatchException` and crashes the program. We'll learn to handle this gracefully in the Exceptions module — for now, assume well-behaved input.

the_newline_gotcha.intro: **THE bug.** Every Java beginner hits this. Read carefully — you'll save yourself an hour of confused debugging.
the_newline_gotcha.the_problem:
**Setup:** you call `nextInt()` to read a number, then `nextLine()` to read a string. The string comes back EMPTY without the user typing anything. Like this:

```java
Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

System.out.print("Age: ");
int age = sc.nextInt();           // user types: 28<Enter>

System.out.print("Name: ");
String name = sc.nextLine();       // never prompts, name = ""

System.out.println("Hi " + name + ", age " + age);  // "Hi , age 28"
```

**Why?** When the user typed `28<Enter>`:
- `nextInt()` consumed the `28` but LEFT the `<Enter>` (the newline character `\n`) in the input buffer.
- The next `nextLine()` saw that leftover newline and immediately returned an empty string. It didn't wait for new input.

In one line: `nextInt()`, `nextDouble()`, `next()`, `nextBoolean()` ALL leave the newline in the buffer. Only `nextLine()` consumes it.
the_newline_gotcha.remember: **Mantra**: 'After every nextInt, throwaway nextLine.' Repeat 3 times.

prompt_pattern.intro: **Use `print` not `println` for prompts** so the cursor sits at the end of the prompt:
prompt_pattern.good_example:
System.out.print("Enter your age: ");
int age = sc.nextInt();
prompt_pattern.good_result: Enter your age: 28      ← cursor was next to the colon
prompt_pattern.bad_example:
System.out.println("Enter your age: ");  // ← println adds a newline
int age = sc.nextInt();
prompt_pattern.bad_result:
Enter your age: 
28        ← cursor jumped to next line — looks weird
prompt_pattern.rule: `println` for things ON their own line (results, headings). `print` for prompts where input follows on the same line.

closing_scanner.intro: Always close your Scanner when done. Two ways:
closing_scanner.explanation: `try-with-resources` declares the Scanner inside `try (...)` — when the block exits (normally or via exception), Java automatically calls `sc.close()`. Safer than manual close (you can't forget). We'll cover try/catch fully in Module 7. For now, manual `sc.close()` at the end of `main` is fine.
closing_scanner.warning: **Don't close `System.in`** in a program that needs more input later — once closed, you can't reopen it. Single-Scanner programs (like everything in this module) are fine to close at the end.

visual_aid.description:
Show a keyboard at top. Arrow flowing down into a box labeled 'System.in (stdin stream)'. Arrow flowing right into 'Scanner sc'. From Scanner, branches to nextInt() (returns 42), nextLine() (returns 'Aisha Khan'), nextDouble() (returns 19.99). Below: a buffer diagram showing the newline bug — user types '28
', nextInt() takes '28', leaves '
' in buffer, next nextLine() instantly returns '' (empty). Bottom: the fix — extra throwaway nextLine() consumes the '
'.
visual_aid.alt_text: Diagram showing Scanner reading from System.in, plus visualisation of the newline-consumption bug and its fix

worked_example.scenario: You're building a 'Loan Eligibility Checker' for a fintech startup. The user enters their annual income, age, and credit score. The program tells them whether they qualify for a personal loan based on simple rules. This is a real fintech feature — every loan app in India (Razorpay, Bajaj Finserv, Paytm) starts with a screen like this.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Write a program that:
1. Asks the user three questions (annual income, age, credit score)
2. Reads each value using the right Scanner method
3. Decides eligibility based on these rules:
   - Annual income must be at least ₹3,00,000
   - Age must be between 21 and 60 (inclusive)
   - Credit score must be at least 700
4. Prints whether they qualify, with reasons if they don't.

Use Scanner. Handle the newline gotcha correctly. Close the Scanner.

step_by_step_solution.details:
Don't forget `sc.close()`. Run the program with sample inputs:

- Income 500000, age 30, score 750 → ✅ qualifies
- Income 200000, age 30, score 750 → ❌ income too low
- Income 500000, age 18, score 650 → ❌ age + credit both fail

worked_example.expected_output:
Enter your annual income (₹): 500000
Enter your age: 30
Enter your credit score: 750

=== LOAN ELIGIBILITY RESULT ===
✅ Congratulations! You qualify for a personal loan.

common_issues.issue: Mixed nextInt + nextLine gives empty string
common_issues.cause: The newline gotcha — nextInt left the newline in the buffer
common_issues.fix: Add a throwaway sc.nextLine() between the nextInt and the next nextLine. See 'the newline gotcha' section above.

common_issues.issue: Program crashes when user types text instead of a number
common_issues.cause: Scanner throws InputMismatchException when type doesn't match
common_issues.fix: For now, only feed it valid input. Module 7 (Exceptions) covers how to handle this gracefully with try/catch.

common_issues.issue: Prompt jumps to next line before user can type
common_issues.fix: Use System.out.print for prompts. println for results.

industry_examples.company_context: Every CLI tool ever (git, npm, mvn, javac)
industry_examples.use_case: Command-line tools read input either from args[] or interactively. `git commit` opens an editor for the message — same pattern as Scanner.nextLine() under the hood. CLI is the entry point for every developer tool on the planet.

industry_examples.company_context: Backend REST APIs (Spring Boot, every web backend)
industry_examples.use_case: When a Spring controller receives `@RequestBody User`, the framework is doing the JSON equivalent of Scanner: reading bytes from a stream, parsing them, converting to Java objects. The mental model is identical. Master Scanner and you've grasped the input pattern that runs every web service.

industry_examples.company_context: Interactive scripts at every IT job (DBAs, sysadmins, support engineers)
industry_examples.use_case: 'Enter the user ID to deactivate: ' / 'Confirm deletion (y/n): ' / 'Enter date range: ' — every internal tool has Scanner-style prompts. Building a quick command-line utility for your team is a daily task in many roles.

Live coding rounds at HackerRank, Codeforces, LeetCode often use stdin input — the platform pipes test data to your program's System.in. Scanner is the standard way to read it. Knowing the newline gotcha cold prevents a public face-palm in front of an interviewer.

applications.interview_relevance:
Scanner itself rarely appears as a direct interview question, but stdin handling DOES come up in coding rounds. You'll be expected to:
- Read `n` integers from stdin and process them
- Read a grid of values
- Read until EOF or a sentinel value

Common questions you may face:
- 'What's the difference between next() and nextLine()?' — token vs whole line.
- 'Why might nextLine() return an empty string?' — the newline gotcha.
- 'What is try-with-resources?' — auto-closing for AutoCloseable. Scanner is one such resource.

If you can't read input fluently, you can't even GET STARTED on a coding round. This is foundational.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Day-to-day with input:
- Building a quick prototype: hardcode values first (faster), wrap in Scanner once you want to test variations.
- Writing a CLI utility: use a real argument parser (Apache Commons CLI, Picocli) instead of raw Scanner — but the mental model carries over.
- Reading from a file: `new Scanner(new File("data.txt"))` — same API, different source. Module 11 (I/O) covers this.
- In production: rarely raw Scanner. But the pattern (open stream → read → process → close) is everywhere.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Forgetting `import java.util.Scanner;` at the top of the file. The class won't compile.
common_gaps.remediation: Make import-on-line-1 a reflex when you see Scanner. IDEs auto-import; without an IDE, you must remember it.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compilation error: cannot find symbol — class Scanner

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Calling nextLine() right after nextInt()/nextDouble() and getting an empty string. THE classic Java beginner bug.
common_gaps.remediation: Always throwaway-nextLine() between numeric reads and a real nextLine(). Or read everything as nextLine() and parse manually with Integer.parseInt.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: An expected text input is empty without the user typing anything

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using System.out.println for the prompt — cursor jumps to next line before user can type.
common_gaps.remediation: Use System.out.print (no `ln`) for prompts. The prompt should sit right next to where the user types.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Prompt and input are on separate lines

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Forgetting sc.close() at the end. The JVM will clean up eventually, but it's bad practice and triggers IDE warnings.
common_gaps.remediation: Either add sc.close() before main exits, or use try-with-resources for automatic close.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Program crashes when the user types non-numeric input into nextInt().
common_gaps.remediation: For learning exercises, document that input must be valid. For production code, wrap in try/catch and re-prompt — covered in Module 7.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using sc.next() for things like full names — gets only the first word.
common_gaps.remediation: next() is for single tokens. Use nextLine() for anything that might contain spaces.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Second part of a multi-word input goes unread or causes downstream bugs

key_takeaways: Scanner reads input from System.in (keyboard by default). Import `java.util.Scanner` to use it.
THE bug: numeric reads leave the newline in the buffer. Add a throwaway `sc.nextLine()` before the next real `nextLine()`.
Use `print` (not `println`) for prompts so the cursor sits inline.
Always `sc.close()` when done — or use try-with-resources for automatic cleanup.

self_check_questions.question: What does `nextLine()` return that `next()` doesn't?
self_check_questions.answer: nextLine() returns the WHOLE LINE (including spaces) up to the Enter key. next() stops at any whitespace and returns just the first token.

self_check_questions.question: You call `sc.nextInt()` then `sc.nextLine()`. The nextLine returns an empty string. Why?
self_check_questions.answer: nextInt() consumed the digits but LEFT the newline (Enter) in the input buffer. nextLine() then read that lingering newline and returned empty without waiting. Fix: add an extra sc.nextLine() right after nextInt() to discard the newline.

self_check_questions.question: What import do you need for Scanner?
self_check_questions.answer: import java.util.Scanner; — placed at the top of the file, above the class declaration.

self_check_questions.question: Should you use System.out.print or System.out.println for a prompt like 'Enter your age:'?
self_check_questions.answer: System.out.print — without the 'ln'. That way the cursor stays on the same line, right after the colon, so the user's input appears next to the prompt instead of on a new line.

self_check_questions.question: What's the modern (Java 7+) way to ensure your Scanner gets closed?
self_check_questions.answer: try-with-resources: `try (Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in)) { ... }`. Java auto-closes sc when the try block exits, even if an exception is thrown.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 1 is complete! You can now do everything a procedural Java programmer needs: declare variables, use operators, branch with if/else, loop with for/while, and read user input. Module 2 (Methods) is where your code transitions from 'a script that works' to 'engineered software.' You'll learn to break programs into reusable named pieces — the single most important habit in software engineering.
